book_metadata.title: Algorithmic Trading: Winning Strategies and Their Rationale
book_metadata.author: Ernest P. Chan

strategies.name: Linear Mean Reversion on Cointegrated ETF Pair (EWA-EWC)
entry_rules: Compute hedge ratio via Johansen eigenvector on [EWA, EWC, IGE]
Form spread: portfolio = hedge_ratio[0]*EWA + hedge_ratio[1]*EWC + hedge_ratio[2]*IGE
Compute lookback half-life using Ornstein-Uhlenbeck regression: delta_y = lambda*y_lag + noise; halflife = -log(2)/lambda
Compute rolling mean and std of spread over halflife window
Z-score = (spread - rolling_mean) / rolling_std
Position size = -Z-score (linear scaling: more extreme = larger position)
Allocate across legs proportional to hedge ratios
exit_rules: Z-score crosses zero (spread reverts to mean)
Position size naturally reduces as Z-score approaches zero
risk_management: Diversification across 3 ETFs reduces single-name risk
strategies.source_chapter: Chapter 2: Mean Reversion of Prices and Returns

backtest_results.notes: Uses Johansen test for cointegration; includes IGE as third leg to improve cointegration

strategies.completeness: High - full code provided in MATLAB, all parameters derivable
strategies.notes: Author emphasizes Johansen over Engle-Granger for multi-leg cointegration. Half-life method is key innovation for lookback selection.

strategies.name: Price Spread Mean Reversion on GLD-USO (Dynamic Hedge)
entry_rules: Estimate hedge ratio using rolling OLS regression (lookback = half-life)
Form price spread: spread = GLD - hedge_ratio * USO
Compute Z-score of spread using rolling mean/std over half-life window
Enter long spread when Z-score < -entry_threshold
Enter short spread when Z-score > entry_threshold
Position size = -Z-score (linear)
risk_management: Dynamic hedge ratio adapts to changing relationship

backtest_results.notes: Lower Sharpe than EWA-EWC due to weaker cointegration in GLD-USO

strategies.notes: Author uses this to contrast price spread vs log price spread approaches. GLD-USO cointegration is weaker than EWA-EWC.

strategies.name: Bollinger Band Mean Reversion on GLD-USO
entry_rules: Compute spread = GLD - hedge_ratio * USO (dynamic hedge via rolling OLS)
Compute Bollinger Bands: upper = mean + entry_std * std, lower = mean - entry_std * std
Entry threshold (entry_std): 1.0 standard deviations
Go long spread when spread < lower band (Z < -1)
Go short spread when spread > upper band (Z > +1)
Position is binary (full size, not linear scaling)
exit_rules: Exit long when spread crosses above mean (Z > 0)
Exit short when spread crosses below mean (Z < 0)
risk_management: Fixed position size (not scaled by Z-score)
Wider bands = fewer but higher-conviction trades

backtest_results.notes: Better APR than linear version on same pair; Bollinger approach outperforms linear on this pair

strategies.notes: Author compares linear vs Bollinger: Bollinger better APR but can miss gradual reversions. Linear better for smooth mean reversion.

strategies.name: Kalman Filter Mean Reversion on EWA-EWC
strategies.type: Mean Reversion / Pairs Trading / Market Making
entry_rules: Use Kalman Filter to dynamically estimate hedge ratio (state = hedge_ratio)
Observation model: EWA = hedge_ratio * EWC + intercept + noise
Kalman Filter parameters: delta=0.0001 (state transition variance), Ve=0.001 (observation noise)
Compute forecast error: e = EWA_actual - EWA_predicted
Compute sqrt(forecast_error_variance): sqrt_Q
Entry: long spread when e < -entry_threshold * sqrt_Q
Entry: short spread when e > entry_threshold * sqrt_Q
Position size proportional to -e / sqrt_Q
risk_management: Kalman Filter adapts hedge ratio in real-time (no fixed lookback)
Forecast error variance provides natural position sizing
Self-correcting: filter adjusts to regime changes

backtest_results.notes: Best performing mean reversion strategy in the book. Kalman Filter eliminates lookback window selection problem.

strategies.completeness: High - full Kalman Filter equations and MATLAB code provided
strategies.notes: Author considers this the most sophisticated mean reversion approach. Eliminates need to choose lookback period. Can also be used as market-making model (quote around fair value). delta parameter controls how fast hedge ratio can change.

strategies.name: Buy-on-Gap Model (Long SPX Stocks)
entry_rules: At market open, compute gap for each S&P 500 stock: gap = (open - prev_close) / prev_close
Select stocks with largest negative gaps (gapped down the most)
Buy at open the stocks with gaps below a threshold (e.g., gap < -X%)
Use limit orders slightly above open price
exit_rules: Sell at market close same day (intraday hold only)
strategies.source_chapter: Chapter 3: Mean Reversion of Stocks and ETFs

backtest_results.notes: Consistent edge from overnight gap mean reversion in large-cap stocks

strategies.completeness: Medium - concept clear but exact gap threshold not specified precisely
strategies.notes: Author notes this works because overnight gaps in liquid stocks tend to partially revert during the trading day. Related to market microstructure (overnight order flow imbalances).

strategies.name: Short-on-Gap Model
entry_rules: At market open, compute gap for each S&P 500 stock
Select stocks with largest positive gaps (gapped up the most)
Short at open the stocks with gaps above a threshold
Use limit orders slightly below open price
exit_rules: Cover at market close same day

backtest_results.notes: Very high APR but lower Sharpe than long-gap model; likely benefited from 2008 crisis period

strategies.notes: Author warns this may have survivorship bias and borrow availability issues in practice. High APR partially due to 2008 crisis period.

strategies.name: SPY vs Component Stocks Arbitrage
entry_rules: Compute theoretical SPY NAV from component stock prices
Compare to actual SPY market price
When SPY trades at premium to NAV: short SPY, buy basket of components
When SPY trades at discount to NAV: buy SPY, short basket of components
Entry when deviation exceeds transaction cost threshold
exit_rules: Exit when SPY price converges to NAV (spread = 0)

backtest_results.notes: Low APR but very consistent; execution speed critical

strategies.completeness: Medium - concept clear but implementation details sparse
strategies.notes: Classic index arb. Author notes this is increasingly competitive (HFT territory). Edge has eroded significantly since 2008.

strategies.name: Cross-Sectional Mean Reversion (Khandani-Lo Long-Short)
entry_rules: At each rebalance, rank all stocks by prior day return
Go long bottom decile (worst performers yesterday)
Go short top decile (best performers yesterday)
Equal weight within each decile
Dollar-neutral portfolio (equal long and short notional)
exit_rules: Rebalance daily (close all positions, re-enter next day)
risk_management: Diversified across many stocks (50-100 per side)

backtest_results.notes: Strategy suffered major drawdown in August 2007 quant crisis. Referenced from Khandani & Lo (2007) paper.

strategies.notes: Author references the famous August 2007 quant meltdown where this strategy type suffered massive simultaneous drawdowns across many hedge funds. Key lesson about crowded trades.

strategies.name: Intraday Cross-Sectional Mean Reversion
entry_rules: Rank stocks by returns since market open
Go long worst intraday performers
Go short best intraday performers
Rebalance every 30-60 minutes throughout the day
exit_rules: Close all positions at market close

backtest_results.notes: Extremely high returns but requires very low transaction costs and fast execution. Author cautions about capacity constraints.

strategies.completeness: Medium - high-level concept, implementation requires tick data infrastructure
strategies.notes: Author explicitly warns these returns are before realistic transaction costs and market impact. Capacity is very limited. Demonstrates the general principle that higher frequency = higher Sharpe but lower capacity.

strategies.name: AUD.USD vs CAD.USD Forex Pair (Johansen Cointegration)
entry_rules: Apply Johansen test to [AUD/USD, CAD/USD] to find cointegrating vector
Form spread using Johansen eigenvector as hedge ratios
Compute half-life of mean reversion via OU regression
Compute Z-score using rolling mean/std over halflife lookback
Position = -Z-score (linear scaling)
strategies.source_chapter: Chapter 4: Mean Reversion of Currencies and Futures

backtest_results.notes: Works because AUD and CAD are both commodity currencies correlated to resource prices

strategies.notes: Author explains the economic rationale: both AUD and CAD are commodity currencies, so their exchange rates vs USD should be cointegrated. This is a fundamentally-motivated statistical relationship.

strategies.name: AUD/CAD Cross Rate with Rollover Interest
entry_rules: Trade AUD/CAD cross directly (equivalent to AUD/USD - CAD/USD spread)
Compute Z-score of AUD/CAD using rolling mean/std
Enter long AUD/CAD when Z-score < -1
Enter short AUD/CAD when Z-score > +1
Account for rollover/swap interest in P&L
exit_rules: Account for rollover costs in exit timing

backtest_results.notes: Lower returns than the two-leg version due to rollover costs eating into profit

strategies.notes: Important lesson: trading the cross directly is simpler but rollover/swap costs matter. The two-leg version (strategy #10) has better risk-adjusted returns. Author uses this to illustrate the impact of transaction costs.

strategies.name: Crude Oil (CL) Calendar Spread Mean Reversion
markets: Futures: CL (Crude Oil) - near vs far month
entry_rules: Compute calendar spread: CL_near - hedge_ratio * CL_far
Hedge ratio from Johansen or OLS cointegration
Compute Z-score of spread over halflife lookback
Long spread when Z < -entry_threshold
Short spread when Z > +entry_threshold
risk_management: Margin requirements much lower than outright

backtest_results.notes: Calendar spreads have strong mean reversion due to cost-of-carry relationship

strategies.notes: Calendar spreads are naturally mean-reverting because they reflect cost of carry (storage, interest, convenience yield). Author explains the futures pricing model: F(t,T) = S(t)*exp(gamma*(T-t)) where gamma is the roll return.

strategies.name: VIX Futures (VX) Calendar Spread Mean Reversion
markets: Futures: VX (VIX Futures) - near vs far month
entry_rules: Compute VX calendar spread: VX_near - hedge_ratio * VX_far
VIX term structure typically in contango (near < far)
Trade mean reversion of the spread around its average
Long spread when spread is abnormally low (steep contango)
Short spread when spread is abnormally high (flat or backwardation)
risk_management: Calendar spread reduces but doesn't eliminate vol risk

backtest_results.notes: High returns but significant tail risk from VIX spikes (e.g., 2008, 2011)

strategies.notes: VIX term structure is persistently in contango (~80% of the time), providing a structural edge. However, backwardation during crises can cause large losses. Author emphasizes risk management is critical.

strategies.name: VX-ES Cross-Asset Mean Reversion
entry_rules: Compute cointegrating relationship between VX and ES
Spread = VX_return - hedge_ratio * ES_return (negative correlation)
Z-score of spread over halflife lookback
When VIX spikes relative to ES drop (spread too high): short VX, long ES
When VIX drops relative to ES rally (spread too low): long VX, short ES

backtest_results.notes: Exploits the well-known negative correlation between VIX and S&P 500

strategies.notes: VIX and S&P are strongly negatively correlated. This strategy profits from temporary dislocations in that relationship. Risk: correlation can break during extreme events.

strategies.name: 2-Year Treasury (TU) Time Series Momentum
entry_rules: Compute return over lookback period (250 days)
Compute return over holding period (25 days)
If 250-day return > 0: go long
If 250-day return < 0: go short
Binary signal (fully long or fully short)
risk_management: Low volatility asset (TU has low duration risk)

backtest_results.notes: Low absolute returns but very consistent; TU is low-vol instrument

strategies.completeness: High - very simple strategy, fully specified
strategies.notes: Author uses this as a baseline time-series momentum example. The 250/25 (lookback/hold) is a standard academic parameterization. Low returns due to low volatility of short-duration treasuries.

strategies.name: Brazilian Real (BR) Momentum
strategies.type: Momentum / Time Series / Emerging Market FX
entry_rules: Compute return over lookback period (100 days)
If 100-day return > 0: go long
If 100-day return < 0: go short
Hold for 10 days then reassess

backtest_results.notes: Higher returns than TU due to EM FX volatility and stronger trends

strategies.notes: Demonstrates that momentum works better on trending, volatile instruments. Emerging market FX often trends due to carry trade flows and macro themes.

strategies.name: Copper (HG) Momentum
entry_rules: Compute return over lookback period (40 days)
If 40-day return > 0: go long
If 40-day return < 0: go short
Hold for 40 days then reassess

backtest_results.notes: Commodity momentum driven by supply-demand cycles

strategies.notes: Shorter lookback/hold than academic standard (250/25) works better for commodities. Author suggests commodity trends are shorter-lived than financial asset trends.

strategies.name: Roll Return Signal for TU Momentum
entry_rules: Compute roll return: gamma = (near_price - far_price) / near_price / (T_far - T_near)
If gamma > 0 (backwardation / positive carry): go long
If gamma < 0 (contango / negative carry): go short
Roll return serves as momentum signal
exit_rules: Signal reversal when roll return changes sign
risk_management: Roll return is a structural signal (cost of carry)
Less prone to false signals than price momentum

backtest_results.notes: Very high Sharpe for the low APR; roll return is a very clean signal

strategies.notes: Key insight: futures roll return (gamma) encodes information about supply/demand and carry. Positive gamma (backwardation) = excess demand for near-term delivery = bullish. This is related to the 'carry trade' concept. Author's formula: F(t,T) = S(t)*exp(gamma*(T-t)).

strategies.name: XLE-USO Roll Return Arbitrage
markets: ETFs/Futures: XLE (Energy Sector), USO (Oil ETF), CL (Crude Futures)
entry_rules: Compute CL roll return (gamma) from futures term structure
When CL in backwardation (positive gamma/positive roll return): long XLE, short USO
When CL in contango (negative gamma/negative roll return): short XLE, long USO
Rationale: USO suffers from contango roll losses; XLE (equities) does not
exit_rules: Rebalance monthly or at roll dates
risk_management: Partially hedged (energy sector exposure on both sides)
Basis risk between XLE and USO
Structural edge from ETF roll cost

backtest_results.notes: Exploits structural disadvantage of commodity ETFs in contango

strategies.notes: Important structural insight: commodity ETFs like USO lose value in contango due to negative roll yield. Energy equities (XLE) don't have this drag. This creates a persistent arbitrage opportunity.

strategies.name: VX-ES Roll Return Strategy
entry_rules: Compute VX roll return from term structure
VX typically in contango: short VX, long ES (harvest contango premium)
When VX flips to backwardation: reverse (long VX, short ES)
Signal based on term structure slope, not price level
exit_rules: Stop loss on VX spikes exceeding threshold
risk_management: Must have stop loss for VIX spikes

backtest_results.notes: Harvests VIX contango premium but exposed to vol spikes

strategies.notes: The VIX contango premium (short vol) is one of the most well-known risk premia in finance. Author emphasizes this is a risk premium, not a free lunch - you are being compensated for tail risk.

strategies.name: Cross-Sectional Futures Momentum
entry_rules: Universe: 20-50 liquid futures contracts across asset classes
Rank all contracts by trailing 12-month return (or 1-month for short-term)
Go long top quintile (strongest momentum)
Go short bottom quintile (weakest momentum)
Equal weight within each quintile
exit_rules: Positions held until next rebalance drops them from quintile

backtest_results.notes: Pre-2008 results. Author notes strategy suffered in 2008-2009 and has had lower returns post-crisis.

strategies.notes: Classic academic momentum strategy (Moskowitz, Ooi, Pedersen 2012). Author warns about post-2008 performance degradation. The strategy is correlated to equity momentum and suffered during risk-off events.

strategies.name: Cross-Sectional Stock Momentum (Jegadeesh-Titman)
entry_rules: Rank all stocks by trailing 12-month return (skip most recent month)
Go long top decile (winners)
Go short bottom decile (losers)
Equal or value-weighted within deciles
Skip most recent month to avoid short-term reversal
exit_rules: Exit when stock drops out of decile

backtest_results.notes: Pre-2008 results. Author explicitly warns these are before transaction costs and may not be achievable. Strategy crashed in 2009.

strategies.notes: Based on Jegadeesh & Titman (1993). Author warns: post-2008 returns much lower. The 12-1 formulation (skip most recent month) is standard to avoid short-term reversal contaminating the signal. Very high Sharpe is pre-cost and pre-2008.

strategies.name: Euro Stoxx (FSTX) Opening Gap Momentum
entry_rules: Compute opening gap: gap = (open - prev_close) / prev_close
If gap > threshold: buy at open (momentum continuation)
If gap < -threshold: sell at open (momentum continuation)
Trade in direction of the gap (unlike mean-reversion gap strategies)

backtest_results.notes: Opening gaps in European indices tend to continue (momentum), unlike US stocks which tend to revert

strategies.notes: Key insight: gaps in FSTX are momentum (continue through the day) while gaps in SPX are mean-reverting. This may be due to FSTX opening after US overnight news is already priced in, creating genuine information-driven gaps.

strategies.name: GBPUSD Opening Gap Momentum
entry_rules: Compute opening gap at London session open
If gap > threshold: buy GBP/USD (momentum)
If gap < -threshold: sell GBP/USD (momentum)
Trade gap continuation
exit_rules: Or close at New York open
strategies.notes: Another gap momentum example. Forex gaps at session opens (especially after weekends) tend to continue. Lower returns than FSTX but still positive expectancy.

strategies.name: Post-Earnings Announcement Drift (PEAD)
entry_rules: Monitor quarterly earnings announcements
Compute Standardized Unexpected Earnings (SUE) = (actual_EPS - consensus_EPS) / std_of_past_surprises
If SUE > threshold (positive surprise): buy stock
If SUE < -threshold (negative surprise): short stock
Enter on first trading day after announcement
risk_management: Event risk: subsequent announcements can reverse

backtest_results.notes: One of the most robust anomalies in academic finance. Exists because investors underreact to earnings news.

strategies.notes: PEAD is one of the oldest and most studied anomalies. Author explains it persists because: (1) institutional investors can't trade small-cap stocks quickly enough, (2) analyst estimates are anchored to prior estimates, (3) behavioral underreaction to new information.

strategies.name: Leveraged ETF Rebalancing Momentum
markets: Leveraged ETFs: DRN (3x Real Estate Bull), DRV (3x Real Estate Bear)
entry_rules: Leveraged ETFs must rebalance daily to maintain target leverage
Near market close (3:45-4:00 PM), predict rebalancing flow direction
If underlying index was UP today: leveraged bull ETF must BUY more (momentum)
If underlying index was DOWN today: leveraged bull ETF must SELL (add to momentum)
Trade in direction of predicted rebalancing flow ~15 min before close
Size based on estimated rebalancing volume
exit_rules: Exit at market close or next day open
risk_management: Rebalancing flows are predictable and mechanical

backtest_results.notes: Very high Sharpe due to predictable mechanical flow. Edge has likely diminished as more traders exploit it.

strategies.notes: Structural edge: leveraged ETFs MUST rebalance daily by buying high and selling low. This creates predictable end-of-day flows. Author notes this was a well-known strategy by 2013 and capacity/edge may have eroded.

strategies.name: Combined Mean Reversion + Momentum on Crude Oil (CL)
entry_rules: Signal 1 (Mean Reversion): Z-score of CL calendar spread
Signal 2 (Momentum): trailing return over lookback period
Combined signal = weighted average of both signals
If both signals agree: full position
If signals disagree: reduced position or flat
Weight allocation between MR and momentum based on recent regime
risk_management: Position size reflects conviction (agreement between signals)

backtest_results.notes: Combining MR and momentum on same instrument reduces drawdowns vs either alone

strategies.completeness: Medium - author discusses concept but exact combination method varies
strategies.notes: Author advocates combining mean reversion and momentum signals on the same instrument. Different timeframes favor different approaches: short-term (mean reversion), long-term (momentum). The combination is more robust than either alone.

strategies.name: Kelly Criterion Optimal Leverage
entry_rules: Not a trading strategy per se, but a position sizing framework
For continuous returns: f = m / s^2 where m = mean excess return, s^2 = variance of returns
For discrete bets: f = (p*b - q) / b where p = win prob, q = loss prob, b = win/loss ratio
Apply half-Kelly (f/2) in practice for safety
Compute f on rolling basis using recent returns
exit_rules: Reduce leverage when Kelly fraction decreases
risk_management: Half-Kelly reduces risk of ruin dramatically
Full Kelly maximizes geometric growth but with high variance
Kelly assumes known and stationary distribution (rarely true)

backtest_results.notes: Not a standalone strategy. Author demonstrates that half-Kelly applied to any strategy improves risk-adjusted returns vs fixed sizing.

strategies.notes: Author strongly advocates half-Kelly or less. Full Kelly is too aggressive for real trading because: (1) return distributions are not stationary, (2) fat tails increase risk of ruin, (3) parameter estimation error means true Kelly is unknown. The continuous formula f = m/s^2 is elegant and easy to implement.

strategies.name: CPPI (Constant Proportion Portfolio Insurance)
entry_rules: Set a floor value (maximum acceptable loss, e.g., 80% of initial capital)
Cushion = portfolio_value - floor
Multiplier = desired leverage when fully invested (e.g., 5)
Risky_allocation = min(multiplier * cushion, portfolio_value)
As portfolio drops toward floor, allocation to risky assets decreases automatically
exit_rules: If portfolio hits floor: 100% in risk-free asset (locked out)
Rebalance allocation continuously as portfolio value changes
risk_management: Risk: gap risk can breach floor in extreme moves

backtest_results.notes: Framework, not standalone strategy. Reduces max drawdown at cost of capping upside in strong trends.

strategies.notes: CPPI provides a systematic way to implement drawdown control. Author notes the tradeoff: you give up upside in exchange for drawdown protection. The 'lock-out' effect (hitting the floor) can be mitigated by using a partial floor reset.

strategies.name: HFT Ratio Trade (Market Making)
entry_rules: Maintain bid and ask quotes around estimated fair value
Fair value estimated from order flow imbalance and recent trades
Buy on bid, sell on ask, capture spread
Adjust quotes based on inventory: if long, lower ask to reduce inventory
Cancel and re-quote when order flow suggests adverse selection
exit_rules: Inventory management: flatten by end of day
Cancel stale quotes immediately when conditions change
Exit all positions if volatility spikes
risk_management: Speed of cancellation is primary risk control
Adverse selection detection (cancel when informed flow detected)

backtest_results.notes: No specific backtest results. Author discusses conceptually. Requires co-location and sub-millisecond execution.

strategies.completeness: Low-Medium - conceptual discussion, not implementable from book alone
strategies.notes: Author provides a high-level overview of HFT market making. Not implementable without: (1) co-location, (2) direct market access, (3) FIX protocol implementation, (4) sub-millisecond infrastructure. Included for completeness but not practical for retail traders.

strategies.name: Momentum Ignition Detection / Stop Hunting
entry_rules: Detect clusters of stop-loss orders at known technical levels
Place aggressive orders to push price through stop cluster
Profit from cascading stop-loss execution (momentum ignition)
Alternatively: detect when others are doing this and trade with them
exit_rules: Exit once stop cascade is exhausted
risk_management: Regulatory risk (momentum ignition may be illegal in some jurisdictions)
Extremely short holding period limits loss

backtest_results.notes: No backtest results provided. Conceptual discussion only.

strategies.notes: Author discusses this as an HFT phenomenon to be aware of, not necessarily to implement. Relevant for understanding why stops get hit at technical levels. Regulatory scrutiny has increased significantly since publication.

strategies.name: News Sentiment Momentum
entry_rules: Process news articles/headlines using sentiment analysis (NLP)
Compute sentiment score for each stock
Buy stocks with positive sentiment momentum (improving sentiment)
Short stocks with negative sentiment momentum (deteriorating sentiment)
Combine with price momentum for confirmation
risk_management: Sentiment can be noisy - use aggregation
Combine with price signals for confirmation

backtest_results.notes: Author references academic research showing sentiment predicts returns. No specific backtest from author.

strategies.notes: Author discusses news sentiment as a momentum factor. References research by Tetlock (2007) and others. Practical implementation requires: news feed, NLP pipeline, sentiment model. More relevant now (2026) with modern LLMs than when written (2013).

cross_cutting_themes.mean_reversion_vs_momentum: Author argues most instruments exhibit BOTH mean reversion and momentum at different timeframes. Short timeframes (intraday to days) tend to mean-revert. Long timeframes (weeks to months) tend to trend. The key is matching strategy type to timeframe.
cross_cutting_themes.stationarity_testing: Before trading any mean reversion strategy, MUST test for stationarity using: (1) ADF test, (2) Hurst exponent (H < 0.5 = mean reverting), (3) Variance ratio test, (4) Half-life of mean reversion. If half-life is too long (>100 days), the strategy is impractical.
cross_cutting_themes.cointegration_over_correlation: Correlation is not sufficient for pairs trading. Two assets can be highly correlated but NOT cointegrated (they can drift apart permanently). Cointegration means the spread is stationary and will revert. Always use Johansen or CADF test, not just correlation.
cross_cutting_themes.roll_return_as_signal: Futures term structure (contango vs backwardation) is a powerful signal. Roll return captures supply/demand information that spot price alone misses. Backwardation = bullish, Contango = bearish for the underlying.
cross_cutting_themes.transaction_costs_matter: Author repeatedly emphasizes that transaction costs (commissions, slippage, market impact) can destroy strategies that look profitable on paper. Higher frequency = more costs. Always include realistic costs in backtests.
cross_cutting_themes.capacity_vs_sharpe_tradeoff: Higher frequency strategies have higher Sharpe ratios but lower capacity (can't manage large AUM). Lower frequency strategies have lower Sharpe but can manage billions. Retail traders should exploit the high-Sharpe, low-capacity niche.
cross_cutting_themes.regime_changes: Many strategies that worked pre-2008 failed post-2008. Author warns about: (1) crowded trades (2007 quant crisis), (2) correlation breakdowns during crises, (3) structural market changes (HFT, regulation). Walk-forward testing is essential.
cross_cutting_themes.kelly_criterion: Use half-Kelly or less. Full Kelly maximizes geometric growth but has unacceptable drawdown risk. The formula f = m/s^2 is simple and powerful but assumes stationary returns (unrealistic).
cross_cutting_themes.futures_pricing_model: F(t,T) = S(t) * exp(gamma * (T-t)) where gamma is the annualized roll return. Positive gamma = backwardation = bullish. This model is fundamental to understanding futures vs spot returns.

key_concepts.adf_test: Augmented Dickey-Fuller test for stationarity. Null hypothesis: series has a unit root (non-stationary). Reject null (p < 0.05) to confirm mean reversion.
key_concepts.hurst_exponent: H < 0.5: mean reverting, H = 0.5: random walk, H > 0.5: trending. Computed via variance of lagged differences.
key_concepts.variance_ratio_test: VR(q) = Var(q-period return) / (q * Var(1-period return)). VR < 1: mean reverting, VR = 1: random walk, VR > 1: trending.
key_concepts.half_life: Time for spread to revert halfway to mean. Computed from OU regression: delta_y = lambda * y_lag + noise. Halflife = -log(2) / lambda. Used as lookback for rolling statistics.
key_concepts.johansen_test: Tests for cointegration among multiple time series simultaneously. Returns eigenvectors (hedge ratios) and eigenvalues (strength of cointegration). Superior to Engle-Granger for multi-leg strategies.
key_concepts.ornstein_uhlenbeck: Mean-reverting stochastic process: dX = theta * (mu - X) * dt + sigma * dW. theta = speed of reversion, mu = long-run mean. Used to model spread dynamics.
key_concepts.kalman_filter: Recursive state estimation. Dynamically estimates hedge ratio without fixed lookback window. State equation: hedge_ratio(t) = hedge_ratio(t-1) + noise. Observation: y = hedge_ratio * x + noise.
key_concepts.sharpe_ratio: Sharpe = (annual_return - risk_free_rate) / annual_std. Author targets Sharpe > 1.0 for viable strategies, > 2.0 for excellent.
key_concepts.maximum_drawdown: Largest peak-to-trough decline. Author considers >25% unacceptable for most strategies.
key_concepts.profit_factor: Not explicitly used by author (prefers Sharpe), but implied in discussion of win rate and payoff ratio.

analysis_metadata.analyst_notes: This book is highly technical and quantitative. The strongest strategies are the mean reversion pairs (especially Kalman Filter) and the roll return momentum strategies. The HFT section is conceptual only. Most strategies require daily or lower frequency data and are implementable by retail algo traders with basic infrastructure.
